data.title: GABF: Best Of Breed Financial Equities ETF
data.description:
GABF strategy

Gabelli Financial Services Opportunities ETF (NYSEARCA:GABF) is an actively managed ETF launched on 5/10/2022 with an objective of capital appreciation. GABF has a portfolio of 40 stocks and a dividend yield of 4.28%. Based on the prospectus, the total expense ratio is only 0.10%, corresponding to acquired fund expenses. Indeed, the 0.90% management fees are totally waived by the Adviser (possibly temporarily).

As described by Gabelli, to be eligible, a company must devote at least 50% of its assets to, or derive at least 50% of its revenues from financial services. The fund applies a proprietary methodology known as Private Market Value with a Catalyst™. Eligible companies are selected based on factors such as price, earnings expectations, earnings and price histories, balance sheet characteristics, and perceived management skills, also taking into account economic and political outlooks. Then, only companies with a catalyst are added in the portfolio.

The portfolio turnover was 31% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the largest capital-weighted ETF in the sector: Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF).

Portfolio

GABF is almost exclusively invested in U.S. companies (about 95% of asset value), with about 49% in large and mega caps, 30% in mid-caps and 21% in small and micro caps. Compared to the sector benchmark, the portfolio is overweight in the capital markets industry (44.1%).

The portfolio is very concentrated: the top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 49.8% of asset value, and each of them weighs between 4% and 7%.

Fundamentals

GABF is slightly more expensive than XLF based on price/earnings, and cheaper for price/book and price/sales ratios, as reported in the next table. Aggregate growth rates are also mixed. GABF doesn’t stand out for its fundamental metrics, which anyway are quite unreliable in the financial sector.

Performance

The track record of GABF is too short to assess its strategy, but it is very encouraging. The fund has outperformed XLF by 8.5 percentage points in annualized return since 5/17/2022, with a slightly higher risk measured by maximum drawdown and volatility.

GABF has little assets under management and weak liquidity compared to peers, but also shows the lowest fees, and the best return and Sharpe ratio (risk adjusted performance) over the last 3 years.

Takeaway

Gabelli Financial Services Opportunities ETF has a portfolio of 40 financial stocks, selected based on fundamentals and catalysts. GABF is quite concentrated in the capital markets industry and in its top holdings. The track record of GABF is too short to assess its strategy, but it is promising. Since its inception 3 years ago, GABF has outperformed the sector benchmark and its competitors, making it more attractive than most, if not all other financial equities ETFs. The fund's low AUM and trading volumes are notable risks, though.

data.title: Workday, Inc. 2026 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Workday, Inc. in conjunction with their 2026 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Vital Energy: Hedges Protect Its 2025 Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Vital Energy, Inc. (NYSE:VTLE) is now projected to generate around $267 million in 2025 free cash flow at current strip. This is only modestly lower than what I had projected for Vital before oil prices (CL1:COM) dropped in April.

Vital is 94% hedged on oil in 2H 2025, so changes in oil prices don't really have any net impact on its free cash flow then.

Vital's hedging position is much smaller in 2026 though, so it is relying on service cost decreases to reduce its corporate breakeven level. If service costs decrease by 10%, it may be able to generate close to $150 million in 2026 free cash flow at current strip.

An indefinite $60 WTI oil environment would likely leave Vital's equity with quite limited value. I am modeling a scenario where oil rebounds to $70 by 2027 though. That scenario should allow Vital to manage its debt and I'd estimate its value at around $34 per share in that scenario (if it isn't forced to dilute to reduce its net debt while oil prices are lower).

Q1 2025 Results

Vital reported relatively strong Q1 2025 production results. During the quarter it averaged 140,159 BOEPD in total production, including 64,893 barrels per day of oil production. This was 2% above the midpoint of its total production guidance and 1% above the oil production guidance midpoint for the quarter.

Vital also expected $115 million to $120 million in lease operating expenses in Q1 2025 and ended up well below that at $103 million. Vital attributed that to lower workover activity during the quarter as well as expenses with its Point Energy assets coming in lower than previously estimated. There was also a $6 million adjustment related to previous periods that reduced its reported lease operating expenses in Q1, so Vital's lease operating expenses would have been close to $110 million without that adjustment.

Vital is taking a relatively conservative approach in projecting its future lease operating expenses and is maintaining its expectations at around $115 million per quarter for the rest of 2025. This allows for the impact of increased water volumes during the rest of the year.

Reagan County Divestiture

Vital made a minor divestiture of non-core Reagan County assets for $20.5 million in cash. This involved 9,100 net acres with current production of 1,300 BOEPD (12% oil).

The divested acreage didn't include any of Vital's inventory locations and appears to part of the gas-weighted legacy position that it stopped developing years ago after starting its acquisitions of oil-weighted inventory.

The divested assets can probably generate around $4 million to $5 million in EBITDA per year (with a low base decline rate) at $60 to $70 WTI oil and assuming a realized price of $12 per BOE for the non-oil production.

Vital also noted that its asset retirement obligation was reduced by $8.4 million from the sale.

Notes On Hedges

Vital is heavily hedged on oil and gas for the rest of 2025. It has swaps covering around 83% of its Q2 2025 oil production and approximately 94% of its 2H 2025 oil production. After factoring in the effect of production taxes, oil price changes during the second half of 2025 would have effective zero direct effect on Vital's free cash flow.

Vital is also heavily hedged on natural gas and propane and ethane for the rest of 2025, so it is only the other NGL components (butane, isobutane and pentanes/natural gasoline) that it has significant price exposure to during 2025. Those other NGL components only account for around 6% to 7% of Vital's total production.

Vital currently has a lot more exposure to commodity prices changes in 2026. It is around 17% hedged on 2026 oil production if production growth is flat compared to 2025, although it seems likely that Vital's oil production will decline at least a little in 2026 if oil remains around $60.

It is also unhedged on NGLs in 2026, although it does have hedges covering approximately 56% of its 2026 natural gas production (based on 2025 production levels).

Updated 2025 Outlook

Vital has maintained its guidance for 2025 so far. Its production outperformance should offset the impact of its Reagan County divestiture, so I am continuing to model its 2025 production at 137,500 BOEPD, including 64,500 barrels per day in oil production.

Vital appears more likely to reduce development starting late in 2025 to early 2026 if oil prices don't improve noticeably by then.

The current strip for 2025 is around $63 to $64 WTI oil (including roughly $61 WTI oil during the last three quarters of 2025).

I project Vital's 2025 revenues at $2.071 billion at current strip prices, inclusive of hedges.

Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 23,542,500 $64.25 $1,513
NGLs 13,505,000 $15.75 $213
Natural Gas 78,840,000 $1.50 $118
Hedge Value $227
Total Revenue $2,071

This results in a projection of $267 million in 2025 free cash flow for Vital.

$ Million
Lease Operating Expense $460
Production and Ad Valorem Taxes $120
Marketing, Gathering and Transportation $74
Cash G&A $90
Net Interest $185
Capital Expenditures $875
Total Expenses $1,804

Debt Situation

Vital expects to put approximately $300 million towards debt reduction in 2025, including the proceeds from its Reagan County divestiture. This would reduce Vital's net debt to around $2.14 billion by the end of 2025, consisting of $1.6 billion in notes and $540 million in credit facility debt (net of cash).

Vital's credit facility borrowing base was reduced from $1.5 billion to $1.4 billion, but Vital still has plenty of room under that facility.

Vital mentioned that it believed its corporate breakeven level could be reduced to $53 oil in 2026 if service costs come down 10% in a continued $60 oil environment. This could allow Vital to generate near $150 million in free cash flow in 2026 at $60 oil.

I believe Vital would be better off by reducing its development pace in 2026 if oil remains around $60 though, so that it can preserve more of its inventory and further increase its free cash flow.

Vital's leverage could end up around 1.8x at the end of 2026 at $60 WTI oil. This is higher than ideal, but at least its largest note maturity isn't until 2032.

Notes On Valuation

I had previously estimated Vital's value at $47 per share at long-term (after 2025) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I have reduced its estimated value to $34 per share now, based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

The reduction in estimated value is due to the $5 decrease in the long-term oil price as well as the assumption that lower oil prices continue through 2026.

Vital's estimated share value is more than double its current $15 share price. Debt makes up the majority of Vital's enterprise value though. After factoring in its net debt, an estimated value of $34 is around 25% to 30% higher in terms of enterprise value than a $15 share price.

Conclusion

Vital Energy's operational performance has been solid and it is very heavily hedged for 2025. This allows it to still generate around $267 million in projected free cash flow in 2025 despite oil strip prices dropping to around $60 for the majority of the year.

Vital expects service costs to come down by around 10% in a continued $60 WTI oil environment, which would then allow it to generate close to $150 million in 2026 free cash flow at $60 WTI oil despite limited hedges.

Vital's leverage is significant at $60 WTI oil, though, and a situation with indefinite $60 WTI oil would leave quite limited value for its equity. Vital does have a bit of time for oil prices to rebound though, with its next note maturity in 2029 and its largest note maturity coming in 2032.

I am modeling a scenario where oil prices remain at around $60 until the end of 2026 and then rebound to $70 after that time. I believe that Vital can be worth around $34 per share in that scenario.

data.title: Motorcar Parts Of America: New Margins, Old Problems (Ratings Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Motorcar Parts of America (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:MPAA) is one of those legacy suppliers in the auto aftermarket that’s been around long enough to seem dependable by default. It makes the essential components like starters, alternators, brake systems, diagnostics, sold to both retail chains and pro installers across North America and abroad. The company has a global manufacturing footprint and a foothold in the kind of vehicle maintenance that can’t really be deferred.

And yet, for all that baked-in relevance, MPAA has spent years stuck in a loop of operational drag and underwhelming returns. Stable on the surface, inefficient underneath.

Last year, I gave a “Sell” rating on MPAA, based on what I saw as a long history of financial underperformance, declining earnings, and a stock price that had cratered since 2016 with no dividends to offset the pain. Despite some encouraging signs like expanding product lines and international growth, I felt the risks far outweighed the potential upside. That call, as it turns out, aged badly. One of my worst.

Since then, the stock’s roared back, a move that made it clear I misread both the company’s staying power and the market’s appetite for a turnaround. It was a clean miss and a reminder of how easy it is to get the timing wrong when a perennial underperformer starts to get its act together. What’s changed is substance: MPAA has restructured its operations, expanded into higher-margin categories, and deployed capital with newfound discipline, suggesting this is no longer a company struggling to stay afloat, but one building a foundation for sustained value creation.

Missed the Mark

Back in October 2024, when my “Sell” call dropped, I thought the rationale was airtight: this was a company with a massive stock collapse, no dividend to distract from the bruises, and a record of earnings volatility that made forecasting an exercise in futility. Despite its global footprint and its portfolio of seemingly indispensable automotive aftermarket parts like starters, alternators, calipers, pads, MPAA looked like it was jogging in place, getting nowhere while its cost base ballooned and consumers pulled back amid macroeconomic malaise.

Yet, since that nadir, the stock has come back viciously, much to the chagrin of my ego, over 90%. That number demands interrogation because the business fundamentals have changed. There’s been a measurable realignment under the hood, and that starts with earnings.

In Q3 of fiscal 2025, MPAA reported $186.2 million in revenue, a year-over-year jump of 8.3%. Not only did this beat consensus by $6.68 million, but it did so in a quarter that typically lags due to seasonal automotive demand patterns.

Gross profit climbed to $44.9 million, a year-over-year gain of 49.4%. Just as crucially, the company’s gross margin jumped from 17.5% to 24.1%, this despite $3.4 million in non-cash charges tied to inventory revaluation and amortization.

Leaner Operations

How did they pull that off? The headline move was operational relocation: shifting production and distribution from Torrance, California to Mexico. That decision slashed overhead, introduced more automation, and allowed for tighter cost control without throwing new capital at the problem. As a result, MPAA generated $34.4 million in cash flow from operations in the quarter. They didn’t hoard it either. They reduced net bank debt by 26%, cutting it from $114.3 million to $84 million. On top of that, they bought back 268,130 shares for $2.1 million at an average price of $7.82.

Then there’s the portfolio expansion. Early 2025 saw MPAA add 120 new part numbers, expanding its coverage to an additional 30 million vehicles in operation. The company’s offerings like alternators, starters, brake calipers, pads, and rotors are being pushed under the Quality-Built and Pure Energy banners, both of which have real cachet in the repair shop ecosystem. Take the brake segment. This used to be a supplementary play, but now it’s MPAA’s second-largest category. The brake caliper facility, which wasn’t even operating at scale initially, has outperformed projections. Its effective capacity has grown by more efficient processes. The result: rising output, improving margins, and zero need for major new capital.

The rotating electrical segment like alternators and starters remains the bedrock. These are parts you replace when they break, not when you feel like it. As the average age of a U.S. vehicle reaches 12.8 years, these replacements become unavoidable. They don’t depend on economic confidence or discretionary spending. If your starter dies, your car is a paperweight.

Diagnostic Future

Yet perhaps the most forward-looking strategy is MPAA’s foray into diagnostics. The JBT-1 benchtop tester is the spearhead here, a diagnostic device aimed at repair professionals. Management’s ambition is to turn this segment into a $100 million revenue generator. But what makes it strategically potent is the recurring revenue potential from software updates, maintenance, and service contracts. This shift toward a SaaS-like margin profile could offer a hedge against the very earnings volatility that torpedoed MPAA’s credibility in past years.

Meanwhile, the Dixie brand is pushing MPAA into the heavy-duty aftermarket: tractors, big rigs, transit fleets, construction equipment. These are high-barrier sectors: low volume, high value, and big headaches for competitors. By establishing a foothold here, MPAA is insulating itself from the commodified chaos of traditional auto parts retail. Moreover, its expanded Mexico footprint adds another layer of risk mitigation. Revenue generated in pesos is now being reinvested locally, reducing reliance on currency hedges and forward contracts. This acts as a buffer against global FX volatility, especially as dollar strength and trade policy shifts create new fault lines.

Finally, there’s the question of geopolitical and supply chain resilience. MPAA’s manufacturing triad of the U.S., Mexico, and Malaysia gives it the kind of geographic diversification that many rivals lack. It’s strategic insurance.

MPAA’s Valuation

At a blended P/E ratio of 16.62x, MPAA trades like a terminal value fossil. But its fair value estimates stretch from $67.13 all the way to $157. Assuming consensus forecasts hold even partially, the implied annual return tops 169%.

That’s lottery ticket territory.

By March 2025, earnings per share are expected to grow by 29.3%, followed by a massive 200% surge by March 2026. If these gains materialize, they’ll compress the forward P/E from 21.26 in 2025 to just 7.09 by 2026, a triple-digit shift without multiple contraction.

And yet, the engine sputters when the hood is lifted. For all the bullish projections, the company posted a trailing twelve-month net loss of $17.4 million.

At the same time, it reported $27.1 million in operating cash flow. The catch? $17.4 million of that came from deferred revenue. That's money counted as cash but not yet earned. This allows MPAA to maintain a semblance of cash health while continuing to bleed at the net level.

The company's levered free cash flow for the TTM, which was, for all intents and purposes, flat. That might not sound damning, until you realize this follows a steep FCF decline in 2023, and a longer trendline of negative free cash flow across most of the past decade. In TTM, capital expenditures were kept to a frugal $2.3 million, yet even this restraint didn’t translate to positive FCF. The reason’s not hard to spot: MPAA’s working capital like receivables, inventory, and payables are shifting unpredictably and making it hard to pin down anything resembling a reliable cash cycle. What looks like discipline starts to feel more like drift.

Quarterly results, in theory, should provide some clarity. In Q3 of fiscal 2025, MPAA did post a profit: $2.3 million in net income, or $0.11 per diluted share. However, this came up short of expectations.

EPS clocked in at $0.35, missing consensus by $0.08. Once again, the gap is largely explained by $5 million in non-cash charges. That persistent interference continues to cloud the picture of whether the company is actually making money or just creating the illusion of it through accruals and adjustments.

FX and Cost Pressures

Let's go back to FX exposure, which might actually be the most glaring red flag. In the same quarter, MPAA recorded a $2.5 million non-cash foreign exchange loss, a reversal from the $3.1 million gain it posted in the same period a year earlier. This was driven primarily by the remeasurement of U.S. dollar-denominated lease liabilities in its Mexican operations, specifically $1.9 million attributed to lease liability adjustments and another $585,000 from losses on peso forward contracts. These currency shocks shaved $3.4 million off gross profit and slashed $5 million from net income.

Operating expenses didn’t offer much refuge either. They ballooned from $20.5 million in the prior year to $27.3 million. Even after stripping out the non-cash and FX exposure, they still stood at $23.6 million, virtually flat against last year’s adjusted $24.2 million. Revenues may be up, but they are being shadowed closely by cost growth, suggesting that the company is expanding with the parking brake on.

Cumulatively, over the first nine months of fiscal 2025, MPAA has racked up a net loss of $18.7 million, or $0.95 per share. Granted, that’s an improvement over the same period last year, when the company posted a huge $50.6 million loss. But again, nearly half of the current year’s loss, $22.4 million, is tied up in non-cash charges. Add another $3.3 million in one-time expenses, and it becomes evident that profitability is being defined as much by what’s excluded as by what’s earned.

Still, MPAA looks good on paper when benchmarked against its sector. EBITDA growth of 26.7% absolutely dwarfs the Consumer Discretionary sector median of just 1.76%. That would suggest some authentic operational strength, if not for the glaring contradiction that forward EBIT is projected to decline by 11.32%. In other words, we’re simultaneously being told that the company is improving and deteriorating, depending on which end of the income statement you look at.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m upgrading MPAA to a Hold. The turnaround seems real. We have operational improvements, margin expansion, and promising new diagnostics and brake segments all point to strategic progress. But the core financials are still a mess with erratic cash flows, persistent non-cash charges, and FX volatility muddying the earnings picture. So until those stabilize and material earnings growth becomes tangible, I’m not buying, but I’m not selling either.

data.title: Thermon Group Holdings, Inc. 2025 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Thermon Group Holdings, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: The Walt Disney Company: Why Insiders Have Been Selling Around $110
data.description:
DIS Stock: Insiders sell vs. prevailing bullish sentiment

I last covered Walt Disney Company (DIS) stock back on March 5, 2025. In an article titled “Disney Stock Has Gone Nowhere In 10 Years And Will Keep Seesawing”, I rated the stock as a hold. My key concerns include the competitive landscape and its valuation at that time. Quote:

The competitive landscape has shifted with the rise of streaming and social media, diminishing Disney's historical advantages. Despite these challenges, Disney's stock remains overvalued, especially when adjusted for growth rates and compared to peers like Netflix and Comcast.

Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around DIS. In the remainder of this article, I will elaborate on the top 3 on my radar: the prevailing sentiment change, its insider transactions, and also the business updates provided in its FQ2 earnings report (ER). Since my last writing, a strong sentiment has been developing for DIS, as you can easily tell from the following chart. The latest ratings from both Seeking Alpha analysts and Wall Street analysts put the stock squarely in the buy category.

However, the above bullish rating is in stark contrast to the insiders’ activities. To wit, the following Real-Time Insider Transaction Data (provided by DataRoma) shows a domination of sell transactions over the last year. There was only 1 recorded buy transaction for a total of $999,994, executed by Mcdonald Calvin on August 08, 2024. In contrast, 9 sell transactions were disclosed, totaling over $44.96 million. Next, I will explain why I do see good fundamental reasons behind such significant net selling by insiders at DIS, leading to a reiteration of my hold rating.

DIS Stock: Why did insiders sell around $110?

Let me first draw your attention to the prices that the insiders have sold their shares. As seen, most of the sales were executed around a price of $110 in the past years. As a most recent example, DIS Executive Vice President Brent Woodford disclosed the selling of 1,000 shares on May 13, 2025, at a share price of $110.8 each (resulting in a total of $110,845). This price range also coincided with the trading range that has accumulated the largest volume on the open market, as highlighted by the green box in the next chart.

There is a good reason behind the coincidence, in my model. I believe with Disney’s current conditions, a price of $110 has already priced in years of growth and thus leaves little margin of safety. As a reflection, this price translates into a P/E (on a Non-GAAP basis) of about 19x, this is substantially (by more than 55%) higher than the sector median of 12.35. Admittedly, this P/E is heavily discounted compared to its historical average (by about 35% on a TTM basis and more than 53% on an FWD basis). However, I will explain next why its historical average P/E offers very little guidance going forward.

DIS Stock: Profitability pressure

The change in the competition landscape has fundamentally altered DIS’s profitability metrics in the past few years. For example, the next chart clearly illustrates a significant shift in Disney's Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) during 2018~2019. Before 2018, DIS consistently achieved terrific ROCE averaging 35.8% and its ROCE was on an overall upward trend. However, it started to decline sharply with the rise of streaming, which was further worsened by the breakout of the COVID-19 pandemic. The ROCE in the past 4 years averages 17.4% only, less than half of the pre-2018 level.

Looking ahead, I don’t expect a recovery anytime soon with the headwinds the company is facing on the direct-to-consumer (DTC) front. Judging by updates provided in its recent earnings report, the company has been trying various approaches on this front (ranging from various promotions, bundles, to price hikes) but with very limited success in my view. For example, its recent attempt to charge higher rates seems to cause additional customer attrition, and the subscriber base fell 1% during the 2024 December quarter compared to the prior quarter. As such, to echo the point made in the earlier section, I don’t suggest investors rely on its past P/E ratio as a guide for its valuation going forward given the drastically different ROCE and competition environment.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few upside risks are worth mentioning to keep the discussion balanced (after all, I am rating DIS as hold). According to the earnings report, management is taking cost-cutting measures, including plans to slash its workforce and also the reduction of some of its capital programs. These measures should help counter rising operating expenses and help to improve its profitability metrics – at least for the near term. DIS has also been actively investing in quality content and strengthening other parts of its business, such as its Experience division. Notably, it has recently announced its seventh theme park in Abu Dhabi (see the map and news below for more details). Given the area’s incredible popularity as a tourist destination, I expect this new theme part to bolster DIS’s presence in the Middle East and also its profits in the years to come.

New York Post (May 7, 2025): Disney is set to open a theme park and resort in Abu Dhabi — its seventh location and first in the Middle East, the entertainment giant announced on Wednesday. The park will be situated on Yas Island, already a notable leisure destination in the United Arab Emirates that includes prominent attractions such as Ferrari World, Warner Bros. World and SeaWorld Abu Dhabi. The area also boasts the Yas Marina Circuit, famous for hosting Formula 1 events.

All told, the direct trigger for this follow-up analysis on DIS stock is the divergence between the prevailing sentiment and its insider transactions. Insider transaction is an area that is often overlooked by many investors in our experiences, and that is why our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) helps our members monitor it. Of course, insiders can always sell their shares for reasons unrelated to business fundamentals. But in the case of DIS, we believe investors should include the insiders’ activities as part of their investment consideration given the dominance of insider sales, the narrow price range they sold, and also the uncertainties in DIS’ business fundamentals. For us, these considerations have led us to remain on the sideline.

data.title: U.S. Downgrade Then And Now
data.description:
Moody’s Investors Services downgraded United States debt from Aaa to Aa1 last week, on May 16th (Moody’s has rated US debt Aaa since 1919). While these days you can’t say anything is a certainty, we certainly would not characterize the downgrade as a surprise. This follows the downgrade by Fitch Ratings from AAA to AA+ on August 1, 2023, and more importantly, the downgrade by Standard & Poor’s from AAA to AA+ almost 14 years ago, on August 5, 2011. Market reactions have differed greatly.

The first two charts below show the change in the ten-year US Treasury yield and the S&P 500 when the markets opened on Monday, August 7, 2011. You can see the rush to US Treasuries, with the yield on the ten-year treasury dropping from 2.56% on the Friday before the downgrade to 2.32% on close of business Monday, August 8th (this would approximate to a 40-basis-point change today). The ten-year yield continued to decline, with a close of 1.7% in early October before it stayed in a 40-basis-point range before closing the year at 1.88 %. Normally, when a credit is downgraded, yields rise at the margin and prices drop (relative to other bonds). In August 2011 the reaction was counterintuitive. Yields fell sharply, as the US was still considered the safe haven and the sentiment towards Congress was that it was dysfunctional and unable to agree on a fiscal path. Therefore, the markets reached for the world’s most liquid asset (which had been downgraded). Risk off was prevalent in the stock markets as well. The S&P 500 dropped 6.7% on that Monday, August 8th, and by early October was down 8.4% from the pre-downgrade level. However, soon after, the stock market began to rise, buoyed by a Federal Reserve which gave forward guidance that they would keep interest rates low. The S&P 500 finished the year +4.8% from the pre-downgrade level. Clearly, investor expectations mattered and the Fed policy did as well, as there was no logical alternative at the time to US Treasuries as a safe haven. This kept bond yields low and helped equities recover.

What about this time?

Market reactions were much more muted and different from fourteen years ago. The ten-year Treasury bond jumped in yield from a May 16th close of 4.48% to 4.56%. The S&P 500 has had a similar yawn, with Monday showing a rise of less than 1/10% and now down as of Tuesday about 0.8% from Friday’s pre-downgrade close. Why the muted reactions? Well, as with any other such events, the reaction to the first event – in this case, the S&P downgrade in 2011 – produces sharp movements in markets. The subsequent downgrades, by Fitch Ratings in 2023 and Moody’s last week, have diminishing effects. Moreover, it seems the US has come to live with a Congress and Administration that have turned a deaf ear towards deficits. The following chart (thanks to our friends at Bianco Research) shows the US debt as a % of GDP. It’s only been this high during wartime. And the current budget negotiations, as well as tariff discussions, seem to have weighed on investor’s minds as potentially bringing larger deficits and possibly higher inflation, respectively. There are now other robust markets for sovereign debt, such as Germany and Japan, so the US Treasury as the world’s safe haven has not been abandoned, but it has company.

The weeks ahead will be telling as Congress grapples with a budget as well as with tariff negotiations. Our thoughts are that the volatility witnessed in the markets will not be as much as in March and April, when both bond and stock markets were buffeted by tariff talks and Jay Powell’s discussions. The economy should be the telling event.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: I Hit Submit And Everything Changed: Lessons From A New Seeking Alpha Analyst
data.description:
I wasn’t sure I belonged here, but then, for the first time, I pushed that submit button, and everything changed.

My name is Julia Ostian. I'm a 27-year-old economics and business management student, a YouTube content creator, and a Seeking Alpha contributor for almost two months.

Two months ago, when I was writing my first article about a stock I had just covered on YouTube, I was convinced it wouldn’t get approved. Why would it? After all, I was just a student making videos about stocks I analyzed for myself. But sometimes life takes unexpected turns. I got the sweetest editor of all to review my first piece. She helps me out a lot, and we get it together for the publication. Sweet Lord, did it just happen?

It did work out, and it worked out pretty well. I started gaining followers pretty fast, and I saw engagement with my articles grow. Is it luck? Partly, yes. But not only. I realized that "luck" is actually a mix of writing ability, audience engagement, openness to editorial feedback, and the belief that you have something unique to say in this age of information overload.

I'm incredibly honored to get the chance to share my experience with you, dear colleagues, and I hope you find something valuable here, whether you're an experienced contributor, a new author, or even a reader who feels he or she has something to share. I’ll talk about why Seeking Alpha was my first choice, what it is to be a newbie (you need to be ready for a tough editing process), how to choose what to write about, how to balance between earning and getting engagement and followers, and how to not get upset by the hateful comments.

What brought me to Seeking Alpha

My investing journey began when everything crashed in 2020 due to the pandemic. I was 23, curious about investing, the stock market, and why the world economy was in free fall. I had no experience, and no economic education, yet, and was still adjusting to life in Israel after making Aliyah (immigrating to Israel) from Ukraine alone at the age of 19. Life was pretty intense during that time. I was adjusting to a new country, studying Hebrew from scratch, and trying to improve my English at the same time, which wasn’t exactly strong either.

Around that time, I had some experience in social media management, and this is when I met a new friend, my husband today, who was buying oil stocks when oil prices went negative. I had no idea what it meant, but the topic fascinated me, so I suggested we create an investment-focused YouTube channel together. He was the host, and I was everything else behind the scenes. That’s when we discovered Seeking Alpha. We were skeptical about the $300 subscription at first, but after just a week, it paid for itself. We saw how much it impacted our investing decisions, so it's been our go-to platform for nearly five years.

Fast-forward to 2025, I was looking for a student job in social media. I didn’t really want to go back to that field, but at the time, it felt like the only option I had. I applied to dozens of positions, sent out countless CVs, and barely got any responses. It was discouraging, especially because I knew I was capable, just not quite in the right place.

But sometimes, life surprises you. I didn’t land a job in social media. Instead, I ended up doing something that fits me so much better - writing about the market for Seeking Alpha. It turned out to be more fulfilling than any of those jobs I was chasing in the first place.

Onboarding as a newbie: What no one tells you

One morning, I asked myself if my studies and experience were enough to write for Seeking Alpha. I read the contributor requirements and thought, maybe they are, if I have something useful to say.

I had bullet points from my YouTube script I prepared a few days before, and I decided to use them for my research (YouTube content is more story-driven; SA needs more structure and numbers). I was writing that first article for about nine hours straight. I was already after a couple of days of research, so the company and the information weren’t new. But I wanted to make it perfect. I read the guidance for analysts, and I made sure to complete every (even the tiniest) requirement. I was so nervous when I hit that submit button. I kept second-guessing myself, wondering if I was smart enough or if the whole idea was just unrealistic. The wait for the feedback was really long. I think it took about 72 hours to get an answer, but I might be exaggerating… because it felt like an eternity.

I finally got an answer, my heart was beating so fast, I opened the email, and… oh no! An AI detection tool identified my article as containing AI text. How could it happen? I followed all the guidelines so meticulously. I wrote everything myself.

Thankfully, I got the kindest editor, Lavina, who explained that it might have been due to AI tools like Grammarly. I hadn’t used anything besides Google Docs' basic grammar check, but the fact that she didn’t reject the article outright gave me hope. I told her I had no idea how it happened and asked what I could do to fix it. Over the next couple of days, we realized the problem likely came from formatting: Paragraph separator lines, bullet points, and remnants from my original YouTube script.

That’s when it hit me - the original script had been loosely shaped with ChatGPT. I often give it my raw stream of consciousness and ask it to help structure my ideas. I don’t use it for analysis, but even that light touch might have flagged something. Plus, I learned AI detectors can misread dry analytical language or formatting quirks as AI-generated content.

For someone who tends to overthink and aim for perfection, this wasn’t easy. I remember feeling incredibly stressed about the possibility of being blocked from Seeking Alpha. It really got to me at the time. (Editors: please know how intense this can feel for new writers!) But Lavina’s support and her kind words about the article’s quality gave me the motivation to keep going. That experience, though stressful, ended up being the push I needed to write again.

Choosing what to write about

After the excitement of the first article wears off, you start thinking more strategically. Mag-7 stocks are super competitive. Less-covered names can be a great opportunity to stand out, but they sometimes don’t attract many readers. And other tickers might generate a lot of attention, but still earn far less than you hoped.

So, how do I balance it? First, I often check social media (X, YouTube, Reddit, etc.) to see which stocks are starting to gain attention. These platforms help me spot what individual investors are currently focused on. Then I bring those ideas to Seeking Alpha, where the audience tends to be more analytical and focused on long-term fundamentals. It’s a good combination, seeing what's starting to 'hype', and then digging deeper to understand whether the story actually holds up.

Second, I try to focus on less-covered stocks that I have a strong personal opinion about. These types of articles can sometimes perform surprisingly well, both in terms of engagement and earnings. At the beginning of April, for example, I wrote articles about Citigroup and JPMorgan. I enjoy breaking down financial companies, and I felt I had a clear and unique take on both. As a result, they ended up among my top five for the month. Another standout was ZIM Integrated, an Israeli company I know well. It brought incredible engagement because I was able to offer a perspective that most contributors simply wouldn’t have.

Hyped companies are also worth writing about if you can stand out. I try to cover at least one a week. And if an article doesn’t do well this time, it might later, or it might bring attention to your next piece on the same stock, so don’t pass on covering the stock if it didn’t work well for you once.

The real work starts after publishing: Engaging with readers

One of the most important things for content performance is audience interaction, especially in the first few hours. People want to engage with what others are reacting to. That means the earliest readers matter most.

The problem is that the person usually feels awkward being the first to write a comment, so if someone comments early, reply quickly. Ask questions. If you manage to reply in the first few minutes, it will send the signal to other readers that you are online and open for a dialogue. This builds trust and increases the chance people will hit that "follow" button. Engagement is a strategy you want to focus on.

Negative comments and how to deal with them

Not all comments are kind. I’m not talking about thoughtful critiques or when someone points out a mistake. I love those. Here, I mean the personal ones that catch you off guard and question your self-confidence.

By the time I started writing on Seeking Alpha, I had already experienced that kind of negativity (and by the way, Seeking Alpha is one of the safest places out there in terms of negative commentary). Last year, I started speaking in our YouTube videos, and one of them went semi-viral. Within a day, it had thousands of views and a wave of coordinated attacks in the comments. I later found out it had been shared in a private group, encouraging people to spam me because their buying price was much higher than the price right now, and this is their way of "fighting" against stock selloffs. It was intense. I couldn’t sleep that night. It made me feel exposed and full of impostor syndrome.

But here’s what it taught me, and what I carry into my work on Seeking Alpha, and hopefully, you will too: Comments like that aren’t really about you. They’re about someone else’s frustration, fear, or losses. When I get negative comments on Seeking Alpha, I take a moment to check in with myself. If it’s constructive, great, I always reply, even if it's challenging. If it’s a direct attack, I don’t engage. It’s rarely worth it.

What I got right, what I got wrong

I think my biggest strength is clarity. I try to read everything about the company and explain it in the simplest way possible. That doesn’t make the article less valuable for experienced readers. I just believe not every investor wants to Google a new term every two minutes.

I learned this from my university experience, especially in Ukraine, where everything is explained in the most complicated way. Lectures in Israel are so much clearer, and because of this, I enjoy the process much more! Same with investing. If you want to be useful, be simple. People will thank you for it.

My biggest mistake? Trying to say everything in one article. For example, I know Palantir inside out. I’ve followed it for four years, spoken to users and engineers, and studied it in depth. I wrote a monster article - DCF, business, risks, everything. I was working on it for hours, and no one noticed it. Why? Because it left no room for discussion. No space for readers to wonder or respond.

I had no idea why it had happened until I read an article by Cory Cramer, here in Analyst Connect. He suggested not covering everything there is in one article. This is simple but genius advice! Who will engage if you know everything? If you covered everything there was to cover, you didn’t leave room for doubt and questions. Big mistake.

This is why I believe it's very important for us to exchange our experiences!

The end

Writing for Seeking Alpha gave me more than just exposure or earnings. It gave me a sense of belonging in a space I used to think was off limits to people like me - a student, a self-taught investor, someone who didn’t always feel confident speaking up. It turns out, clarity and authenticity matter more than credentials.

So if you’re sitting on an idea, unsure if you’re ready, hit submit. The worst that can happen? You learn something. The best? You find out you belong here more than you thought.

data.title: Tempus AI: I Believe In The Company's Cause
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to have a look at Tempus AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:TEM) which has seen some buzz recently, and see how it has performed recently, and what it is that it offers to the world that makes it a good investment. I believe that the product and the vast dataset on patients and diseases are going to be very valuable to many companies and clinics, so I am willing to break my conservative mindset and see where this company is going to go next. I initiate my coverage of Tempus AI with a Buy rating.

Briefly on Most Recent Performance

Sales in Q1 grew by 75.4% year-over-year to $255.7m, aided by an acquisition that closed in February of this year. Looking at the breakdown in the company’s revenues, Genomics remains the key growth driver and the top revenue contributor with $193.8m in sales, +88.9% y/y growth, driven by oncology testing, which saw sales growth of 31%, ending the quarter at $119m. Hereditary testing made up the rest of the revenue in Genomics, coming in at $63.5m, and +23% unit growth.

The second revenue segment, Data & Services, came in at around $62m, which is around 43% y/y growth, with data licensing seeing a tremendous growth of 58%. The top-line performance was pretty decent in my opinion. Analysts do not expect this performance to continue for the upcoming years, as the revenue growth numbers go down to around 24% for ’26 and ’27; however, that may change if the company continues to acquire more companies, sign partnerships, and get its AI tools to more companies and hospitals.

Now, on to the company’s efficiency and profitability, gross profit came in at $155.2m, which basically doubled from last year, bringing gross margins to 60.6% vs. 53.3% last year. So, we can see the margin expansion was driven primarily by a massive increase in the top line over the year, with costs growing at a much slower pace. That is the trend I would like to see continue going forward. The company’s Adjusted EBITDA came in at a loss of $16.2m, which is a significant improvement over last year’s -$43.9m. The company (on an adjusted basis) is approaching breakeven, with EPS coming in at a -$0.23, which beat estimates by 2 cents.

What about the company’s financial position, which is very important when it comes to a business that is still not profitable? TEM ended the quarter with around $151.6m in cash and equivalents, and $67m in marketable securities, bringing its total liquidity to around $219m, against around $800m in long-term debt, which increased quite a bit since December ’24 due to the acquisition of Ambry.

Is this worrisome? Given the company’s business that is still burning quite a bit of cash, it is a bit worrisome; however, I don’t think it will go bankrupt right after acquiring Ambry. Also, the company expects to be adjusted EBITDA positive by the end of 2025, as well as they see a continuation in margin improvements. On top of that, the cash position should be enough to hold on until it is profitable. The company is paying around $18m in interest expenses, so it is quite a chunk, and if it cannot get to profitability soon enough, I expect investors to flee.

The company is still quite young, and I could see it raising more capital through an equity offering if need be. There haven’t been any filings reported to the SEC regarding an equity shelf offering, but if it has to be done, it will be done. Shareholder dilution is not ideal, however, if the share price outpaces the dilution, then it’s a win-win.

Overall, the results are heading in the right direction all around. However, I expect growth numbers to come considerably down due to the acquisition not bringing in that inorganic growth any longer unless the company manages to continue the growth organically or acquire more companies, which could further hurt its balance sheet, and it is already a bit iffy in my opinion. Nevertheless, I like the direction the company is headed in.

Comments on the Outlook

I have to say, I am a huge fan of companies that work towards the greater good, especially when it comes to combating cancer. So, naturally, my views are going to be a bit biased here. I like supporting companies with such great causes.

I think the company has a lot to offer, given its massive data on a lot of different diseases and treatments. The company has built one of the largest libraries of clinical data as well as molecular data, and through the use of AI-enabled tools and platforms, the vast amount of data can be analyzed at a much quicker speed, which gives more time for doctors and patients. Time that may mean life or death for the patient. The data it gathered throughout the years of operations is going to be very valuable for any company and clinic that specializes in oncology, and as we know by now, data is everything.

So, the company is sitting on a gold mine right now, and with continuous learning, as more data is collected, the AI models get smarter, which means they would be better at identifying genetic mutations in a patient’s tumor or finding biomarkers that can help with finding the most effective treatment, tailored specifically to the patient by using machine learning, the TEM’s platform could be able to predict better how likely the patient is going to respond to different therapies. All this can be done in a fraction of the time compared to human assessments, and that is going to save a lot more lives in the long run.

You may ask yourself, how reliable AI has been for cancer treatment overall, and you’d be right to question. TEM’s AI use in cancer care has shown significant reliability and impact, for both the company and cancer treatment overall.

With one of the most comprehensive and diverse cancer data sets in the world, it was validated at major conferences like ISPOR 2025, which stands for International Society for Pharmacoeconomics and Outcomes Research, which was held in Montreal between May 13th and 16th. Going forward, I am expecting to see TEM’s AI-powered insights to be adopted by many healthcare systems and companies in the future. The company’s AI platforms, like Loop, are helping doctors, clinicians, and researchers to dramatically accelerate the discovery and validation of new drug targets within cancer treatment.

In terms of cancer treatment, studies have shown, which were made by Tempus and its partners, so take that for what it is, demonstrating that AI-driven genomic testing resulted in better patient outcomes. One example given to us was in colorectal patients who received genomic results within two months of biopsy, resulting in significantly better survival rates. This was proven by the Tempus data at the ISPOR 2025 conference.

To succeed, the company needs more partnerships

We already know the company has a lot of partnerships under its belt, with the most recent one being with Verastem Oncology (VSTM), to develop a companion diagnostic test to evaluate VSTM’s ovarian cancer treatment, and Boehringer Ingelheim, which gives the company access to TEM’s massive database. The partnerships with AstraZeneca (AZN) and Pathos should drive the company’s pipeline further and provide decent growth in innovation going forward, translating into better overall company performance.

Around two years ago, the company entered into a strategic partnership with Pfizer (PFE), which gives it access to TEM’s AI-powered platform and the vast library of de-identified, multimodal patient data. Now that Pfizer is one of the bigger players in the oncology game, a continual partnership will be beneficial to the company and cancer treatment overall. By partnering with Tempus, Pfizer aims to make drug development much more accurate and data-driven, which should speed up the process.

More partnerships like this are welcome.

Investor Takeaway

The company is not the type I usually invest in. It seems very risky because of its financials, especially; however, I believe in the company’s cause in battling these diseases, and I am willing to put aside my conservative mindset for the time being, and I am going to start a relatively small position. Because it is a risky investment, the position for now will remain small until I see further financial and operational progress.

I think the company has a lot to offer to these other big players in oncology treatment, and I believe the data it has amassed over the years is very valuable; therefore, I am willing to give the company a go for the time being by initiating my coverage of Tempus AI with a buy rating.

data.title: Euro Lower On Soft Eurozone, German Services PMIs
data.description:
By Kenneth Fisher

The euro is in negative territory on Thursday, following three straight winning days. In the North American session, EUR/USD is trading at 1.1285, down 0.37% on the day.

Services PMIs weaken in May

Business activity in the eurozone fell in May, reflecting the worsening downturn in the services sector. Preliminary Services PMI dropped to 48.9 from 50.1 in April and shy of the market estimate of 50.3. This points to contraction and was the weakest level since Jan. 2024. Business confidence fell to its lowest level since Sept. 2022, as the outlook for domestic and foreign demands remains grim.

Germany, the largest economy in the eurozone, is in even worse shape. The Services PMI dropped to 47.2, down from 49 and below the market estimate of 49.5.

The services sector has traditionally been the engine for growth the eurozone and the eurozone and with services in contraction in both Gemany and the eurozone, a recovery seems a long way off. Manufacturing PMIs showed a slight improvement in Germany and the eurozone in May but both remain in contraction with readings below the 50 line.

The weak PMI numbers provide support for the European Central Bank to lower rates at the June 5 meeting. The ECB lowered rates to 2.25% from 2.4% in April but will have to be cautious in the turbulent economic environment.

US PMIs accelerate

In the US, today's PMI reports were better than expected. Manufacturing rose to 52.3, up from a downward revised 50.8 in April and above the market estimate of 51.0. This marked a three month-high. Services also accelerated to 52.3, up from 50.8 and above the market consensus of 50.8.

EUR/USD Technical

  • EUR/USD has pushed below support at 1.1312 and 1.1298. The next support level is 1.1282
  • There is resistance at 1.1328 and 1.1342

Original Post
